title: Vibing, Harness and OODA loop - by Oskar Dudycz
description: On why Vibing and Harness are not new and why feedback loops are important

title: Yoda Principle for better integrations - by Oskar Dudycz
description: On why doing is better than trying for api and workflows design

title: Anti-patterns in event modelling - Passive-Aggressive Events
description: On why event-driven communication should not be only about events.

title: The one where Oskar explains Example Mapping
description: Learn one of the simplest and actionable modelling technique

title: Interactive Rubber Ducking with GenAI - by Oskar Dudycz
description: On how to work on the design with GenAI, without getting mad at you're absolutely right!

title: The End of Coding? Wrong Question - by Oskar Dudycz
description: Be careful what you wish for, because your wish may come true.

title: Parse, Don't Guess - by Oskar Dudycz - Architecture Weekly
description: Last time, I shared with you how sneaky I was on transaction handling.. Today, the opposite: I’ll tell you how I fixed the issue when I tried to be too sneaky. I already told you that Sneaky Code Bites Back. The moral? Do as I tell, not how I do.

title: How I cheated on transactions - by Oskar Dudycz
description: Or how to make tradeoffs based on my Cloudflare D1 support

title: On rebuilding read models, Dead-Letter Queues and Why Letting Go is Sometimes the Answer
description: In the last article, I explained how to rebuild Event-Driven Read Models in a safe and resilient way. I asked readers to let me know if they find any blind spots in my design.

title: Rebuilding Event-Driven Read Models in a safe and resilient way
description: And what distributed locking, PostgreSQL Advisory Locks have to do with that

title: Architecture Weekly is 5 years old! 🎉 - by Oskar Dudycz
description: Yup, I just realised that it happened! Let's do a small celebration and recap how I got here!

title: Multi-tenancy and dynamic messaging workload distribution
description: This time, I discussed how to setup and scale message processing when you have multi-tenant environment or other dynamic setup. You'll see considerations where to scale horizontally, where vertically and why that matters to you

title: Checkpointing the message processing - by Oskar Dudycz
description: I realised that checkpointing our (message) processing is similar to using cheat codes in Super Frog and similar old games.We need to recover and use the code to get where we were. Of course, sometimes that means going through the half of the level again, but well, better than from the beginning.I also shared with you implementation based on the PostgreSQL how to do it, and how checkpointing can also help in a generic idempotence check (with caution) and to detect noisy neighbours (with understanding).

title: Consumers, projectors, reactors and all that messaging jazz in Emmett
description: Let me show you today how I designed messaging abstractions in Emmett, and why I split them into Consumers and Processors. Why I added abstractions like Projector and Reactor, and the trade-offs I chose. I think this can be a good learning experience on how messaging systems are built and the reasoning behind them!

title: Requeuing Roulette in Event-Driven Architecture and Messaging
description: I’m always saying that there’s a thin line between good and bad practice, and this thin line is named “Context”. That’s also true for the (anti-)pattern I’m calling “Requeuing Roulette”.What’s the Requeuing Roulette? As the name suggests, this technique involves putting a message back into the queue. It also (correctly) suggests that we’re hoping for the best. And sometimes we may be lucky to be true. Let's discuss pros and cons of it!

title: Handling Events Coming in an Unknown Order
description: In the last article we discussed on the scenario where you know what events you should receive, just not the order. But what if you don't know that? That's the question I got from Ben, let's answer that today and explain how using revisions can help you in reaching strict ordering.

title: Dealing with Race Conditions in Event-Driven Architecture with Read Models
description: My events came out of order! What should I do? Are you familiar with the term "phantom record" and its benefits? No? Let me explain it to you today. Let's discuss how to embrace the chaos and learn to deal with it.

title: On Messaging and Distributed Systems with Ian Cooper
description: Why do we still need to learn messaging? Why isn’t it a commodity yet? How to shape boundaries, and how data on the insight, and data on the outside can help in that. This and more we discussed with Ian Cooper learning also how he started his journey and his approach to learning and teaching.

title: New Recording on Event modelling anti-patterns from DDDEU
description: Have you heard about Passive Aggressive Events or CRUD sourcing? Or maybe about the Clickbait event?If you don't, check the recording of my talk, as knowing only best practices is one side of the coin. Knowing what NOT to do can be even more important.You'll learn the specifics of event-driven modelling, starting with worse practices and knowing why and how to avoid them.You'll hear the story of Rakso, who ended up with the distributed monolith and understand how ignoring modelling practices led him to that pitiful place. Thanks to that, you'll see what and how to avoid it in your case.You'll also find out that there's a thin line between bad and good practices: context.

title: Sneaky Code Bites Back - by Oskar Dudycz
description: I was adding SQLite support to Pongo when I realised that I'm talking to myself, and what I heard was insane. I was trying to understand and recap what I just built, an extremely clever and sneaky solution to handle multiple databases. I realised I was wrong, and that sneaky code will soon bite me back. Why?
